critic_raw:
The ATSPrivacy framework for privacy-preserving collaborative learning has similar issues to the Drabent replication (rc_rescience_2021_drabent2022replication_journal) but with distinct additional concerns.

1. The privacy score metric is defined against specific reconstruction attacks that are used during the augmentation policy search. This creates a closed loop: the policy is optimized against the attacks that score it, and there is no guarantee of privacy against novel reconstruction methods. The evaluation should include at minimum one held-out attack not used during search to test generalization of the privacy guarantee.

2. The policy search treats privacy and accuracy as two objectives to be balanced via score thresholds. However, the Pareto frontier of privacy-accuracy tradeoffs is not characterized. The paper reports a single policy per experiment, but there may be policies that achieve substantially better privacy at a small accuracy cost, or vice versa. Without the full Pareto frontier, it is impossible to know whether the selected policy represents a good operating point.

3. The augmentation operations are applied locally before gradient computation. This means the gradients themselves carry information about the augmented (not original) image. A sophisticated reconstruction attack that knows the augmentation policy can apply the inverse transformation to approximately reconstruct the original image. The paper does not test this 'white-box augmentation' attack scenario.

4. The framework's effectiveness depends on the assumption that gradient-based reconstruction attacks cannot invert the augmentation pipeline. For simple augmentations like translations and contrast adjustments, analytical inversion may be possible. For more complex augmentations, learned inversion may be feasible. The paper treats the augmentation as a one-way function without establishing that it has one-way properties under any formal threat model.

5. The accuracy score S_acc is measured on a specific model and dataset pair. It is possible that augmentations that have negligible impact on one architecture (e.g., ResNet) have significant impact on another (e.g., attention-based models that are more sensitive to local spatial transformations). Cross-architecture generalization of the accuracy score is not reported.
all_issues_raised: Privacy score evaluated only against attacks used in policy search; no held-out attack evaluation
Pareto frontier of privacy-accuracy tradeoffs not characterized; single operating point reported
White-box augmentation attack (attacker knows policy, applies inverse transform) not tested
Augmentation is not established as a one-way function under any formal threat model
Accuracy score generalization across architectures not tested
Privacy score measured against a fixed reconstruction attack; adaptive attacks where the attacker knows the augmentation policy are not evaluated
Augmentation operates on images before gradient computation; gradient leakage after augmentation is not analyzed
Policy transferability across datasets not validated for architecture dependence of augmentation effectiveness
Augmentation changes the training distribution, potentially making S_acc an overestimate of deployment accuracy
No comparison to formal privacy-guaranteeing alternatives (DP, secure aggregation) on a normalized privacy level basis
Privacy score is not a formal privacy metric; no guarantee against attacks outside the evaluated family; optimal policies may be attacker-specific
Semantic content preservation by augmentation design does not prevent semantic-level gradient inversion; pixel-level and semantic-level privacy are conflated
Accuracy score is too coarse to detect gradient distribution changes that could alter model vulnerability without changing final accuracy
Semantic-content-preservation interpretation of found policies is post-hoc; generalization to other datasets and architectures is not tested
Reproduction study does not characterize search variance across runs; high-variance policies make original results non-representative
